ABSTRACT 
21 
 
22 
During cell migration and polarization, hundreds of signal transduction and cytoskeletal 
23 
components self-organize to generate localized protrusions. Although biochemical and genetic 
24 
analyses have delineated many specific interactions, how the activation and localization of so 
25 
many different molecules are spatiotemporally orchestrated at the subcellular level has remained 
26 
unclear. Here we show that the regulation of negative surface charge on the inner leaflet of the 
27 
plasma membrane plays an integrative role in the molecular interactions.  Surface charge, or zeta 
28 
potential, is transiently lowered at new protrusions and within cortical waves of

Ras/PI3K/TORC2/F-actin network activation.  Rapid alterations of inner leaflet anionic 
1 
phospholipids, such as PI(4,5)P2, PI(3,4)P2, phosphatidylserine, and phosphatidic acid, 
2 
collectively contribute to the surface charge changes. Abruptly reducing the surface charge by 
3 
recruiting positively charged optogenetic actuators was sufficient to trigger the entire 
4 
biochemical network, initiate de novo protrusions, and abrogate pre-existing polarity.  These 
5 
effects were blocked by genetic or pharmacological inhibitions of key signaling components 
6 
such as Akt and PI3K/TORC2. Conversely, increasing the negative surface deactivated the 
7 
network and locally suppressed chemoattractant-induced protrusions or subverted EGF-induced 
8 
ERK activation.  Computational simulations involving excitable biochemical networks 
9 
demonstrated that slight changes in feedback loops, induced by recruitment of the actuators, 
10 
could lead to outsized effects on system activation. We propose that key signaling network 
11 
components act on, and are in turn acted upon, by surface charge, closing feedback loops which 
12 
bring about the global-scale molecular self-organization required for spontaneous protrusion 
13 
formation, cell migration, and polarity establishment.  
14 
 
 
 
INTRODUCTION 
15 
 
16 
Cell migration and polarity involve large networks of interacting signal transduction and 
17 
cytoskeletal components 1-4.  Several important features of these networks are emerging. First, 
18 
there is an exquisite spatiotemporal coordination of the molecular events: For example, Ras, 
19 
PI3K, and Rac activation along with actin polymerization define growing protrusions at the front 
20 
of the cell while PTEN, RhoA, and myosin II leave these regions and return as the protrusions

are withdrawn.  To the extent that it has been examined, this complementary organization is 
1 
conserved during macropinocytosis, phagocytosis, cytokinesis, within traveling cortical waves in 
2 
different cells throughout phylogeny, and in apical-basal polarity of epithelial cells 5-7. Second, 
3 
consistent with the network concept, multiple genetic, pharmacological, and mechanical 
4 
perturbations on different individual components often result in surprisingly similar phenotypic 
5 
changes suggesting that the overall “setpoint” of the network is more important than any single 
6 
constituent 8-19.  Although much has been gained by studying detailed interactions between 
7 
different molecules, a systems level explanation is needed to account for these emergent 
8 
properties.  It is possible that the features could be a result of a series of stepwise protein-protein 
9 
interactions but it is more likely that the organization depends on a biophysical property of the 
10 
plasma membrane 20.   Such global biophysical organizers have been suggested 1, 11, 20, but 
11 
properties that track closely with the observed dynamic spatial and temporal organization, or that 
12 
allow small alterations to bring about dramatic shifts in the setpoint of the entire system causing 
13 
outsized phenotypic effects, have not been identified.   
14 
One prospective candidate for a such a biophysical organizer is inner membrane surface charge 
15 
or zeta potential.  The cell maintains a negative charge on the inner leaflet of the plasma 
16 
membrane, primarily by regulating the concentrations of multiple anionic phospholipids21-24. Of 
17 
course, numerous studies have focused on specific protein interactions with individual lipids 
18 
such as PIP325, 26 and many cytoskeletal proteins were reported to bear PI(4,5)P2 binding 
19 
motifs27. Interestingly, it has been reported that enzymatic lowering of specific anionic lipids 
20 
such as PI(4,5)P2 or PI(3,4)P2 can lead to the network activation and alterations in cell 
21 
migration9, 28. Relatively few studies have focused on the role of membrane surface charge itself 
22 
21, 29. These have proposed that the zeta potential, in the range of -20 to -50 mV30-32, acts in

regulating local pH and soluble second messenger concentrations, altering ion channel 
1 
conductance, and controlling the association of peripheral membrane proteins 21-24, 33-36. It was 
2 
also shown that the during immune synapse formation and phagocytosis, the membrane surface 
3 
charge changes 22, 37-39. However, it was not envisioned or assessed whether network activation 
4 
and protrusion formation are associated with increases or decreases in surface charge or whether 
5 
direct perturbations of surface charge can activate/deactivate the network.   
6 
 
7 
To address these questions, and to understand the possible role of surface charge as a biophysical 
8 
organizer, we examined whether the surface charge profile changes during cell migration and 
9 
polarity establishment and designed actuators to alter rapidly the surface charge in a particular 
10 
domain of the membrane.  Here, we first showed that PI(4,5)P2 and PI(3,4)P2, 
11 
phosphatidylserine and phosphatidic acid are dynamically lowered during the activation of 
12 
Ras/PI3k/Akt/F-Actin network.  Based on these initial observations, we used a generic surface 
13 
charge sensor to assess whether surface potential tracked with the activation process.  We 
14 
observed that the surface potential is high in resting cells and is transiently lowered during 
15 
network activation and protrusion formation.  Altering surface potential by recruiting our novel 
16 
positively or negatively charged optogenetic actuators to the membrane in a spatiotemporally 
17 
controlled fashion, we establish that the level of surface potential in a local membrane domain is 
18 
not a consequence but sufficient and necessary for activation of the entire signal transduction 
19 
network and directing migration and polarity phenotypes.

RESULTS 
1 
 
2 
 
3 
To analyze the organization of coordinated signaling and cytoskeletal activities, we started by 
4 
examining protrusion formation in migrating Dictyostelium cells as well as cortical wave 
5 
propagation in Dictyostelium and RAW 264.7 macrophage cells (Figures S1A-S1C).  As 
6 
previously noted 3, 5, 26, 40, events that facilitate protrusion formation such as Ras/PI3K activation 
7 
and actin polymerization localize to the front regions of migrating cells (Figures S1A and S1D), 
8 
whereas those that suppress protrusions such as PTEN and myosin II localize to back (Figure 
9 
S1E).  An analogous complementarity was observed in the ventral wave patterns: the facilitative 
10 
and suppressive components dynamically separate into broad, propagating “front-” and “back”-
11 
state regions, respectively (Figures S1F-S1I) 6, 41-43.  The waves have been shown to control 
12 
protrusion dimensions and corresponding modes of cell migration, and thus serve as a useful 
13 
surrogate for “front/back” polarity. To quantitate the extent of correspondence or 
14 
complementarity between the distributions of a pair of different molecular components, we 
15 
developed a conditional probability-based index or CP index (methods; Figures S2A and S2B) 
16 
which was positive or negative, respectively, for co-localized versus complementary-localized 
17 
pairs (Figures S2C and S2D).  For most of the data, both normalized line scans and CP indexes 
18 
are reported to provide quantitative measures of fractional changes in biosensor levels as well as 
19 
the extent of complementarity.

PI(4,5)P2, PI(3,4)P2, PS, and PA dynamically co-segregate to back-state regions of the 
1 
membrane  
2 
 
3 
To gain insight into the spatiotemporal coordination among the signaling and cytoskeletal 
4 
components, we examined several anionic phospholipids in the inner surface of the plasma 
5 
membrane using genetically encoded biosensors. The distribution of PHPLCδ indicated that 
6 
PI(4,5)P2 was depleted in the front-state regions of ventral waves and from protrusions in 
7 
migrating Dictyostelium cells (Figures 1A and 1B; Video S1).  Equivalent patterns of PI(4,5)P2 
8 
were observed in macrophage ventral waves (Figure 1C; Figure S3A; Video S1), as previously 
9 
reported44, 45.  The PI(3,4)P2 biosensor, PHCynA, displayed a similar pattern in the Dictyostelium 
10 
ventral waves (Figure 1D; Figure S3B; Video S2), consistent with our previous reports of its 
11 
depletion from the protrusions28, 46.  Line scans showed around 40-70% less PI(4,5)P2 and 
12 
PI(3,4)P2 in front versus back regions of the waves. The consistently high negative values of CP 
13 
indices for PI(4,5)P2 and PI(3,4)P2 showed that the complementarity with respect to PIP3 was 
14 
maintained during the dynamic patterning (Figures 1E and 1F).   
15 
 
16 
Since these two signaling lipids segregated to the back-state regions of the membrane, we 
17 
wondered whether other major anionic phospholipids, such as phosphatidylserine (PS) and 
18 
phosphatidic acid (PA), exhibit asymmetric patterns.  We studied the spatiotemporal 
19 
distributions of PS, which accounts for ~20% of inner leaflet lipids47, using its biosensor LactC2 
20 
47, 48. Similar to PI(4,5)P2 and PI(3,4)P2, PS localized to the back-state regions of ventral waves 
21 
of Dictyostelium (Figure 1G; Figure S3C; Video S3) and RAW 264.7 macrophages (Figure 1H; 
22 
Figure S3D; Video S3) and was depleted from protrusions in migrating Dictyostelium cells

1 
back-state regions of the ventral waves (Figure 1J; Figure S3E) and away from protrusions in 
2 
migrating Dictyostelium cells (Figure S3F). Line scans showed around 30-50% less PS and PA in 
3 
front versus back regions of the waves. The CP indices of PS and PA were also computed with 
4 
respect to PIP3 (Figures 3K and 3L) and were found to be consistently negative, similar to 
5 
PI(4,5)P2, PI(3,4)P2, and PTEN (Figure S3G). Taken together, these observations show that four 
6 
major anionic lipids decrease in the front-state region of the membrane while PIP3 increases.  
7 
While PIP3 carries the most negative charge, it is a minor constituent47, 51-53.   
8 
 
9 
Higher negative surface charge defines the inactive region or “back-state” regions of the 
10 
cell cortex 
11 
 
12 
Reasoning that, with respect to charge, the decreases in the four predominant anionic 
13 
phospholipids would more than offset any increases in PIP3, we sought to measure 
14 
spatiotemporal changes in membrane surface charge. We started with a generic membrane 
15 
surface charge sensor, R(+8)-Pre, which had been quantitatively characterized in vitro (by 
16 
liposome binding assays) to detect the combination of anionic lipids 22, 30, 54. Consistent with our 
17 
observations of the individual lipids, we found that the back-state regions of cortical waves in 
18 
Dictyostelium cells are defined by high negative surface charge whereas the front-state regions 
19 
shun the charge sensor (Figure 2A; Video S4). Line scans demonstrated around 40-60% less 
20 
charge sensor in front versus back regions of the waves (Figure 2A). Despite continuous 
21 
propagation of front-region waves, as marked by PIP3, R(+8)-Pre faithfully maintained a 
22 
preferential back-state localization (Figure 2B). Similarly, protrusions on migrating cells were

2

3 
 
4 
Importantly, as previously reported for patterns of signal transduction events such as PIP3 
5 
accumulation and PTEN dissociation (Figure S1H) 42, 43, the dynamic distribution of surface 
6 
charge was relatively independent of the actin cytoskeleton. In Latrunculin A-treated cells where 
7 
periodic circulating waves were induced, the charge sensor continuously adjusted its localization 
8 
towards the back-state during 12 minutes of observation (Figures 2F and 2G).  It has been 
9 
previously shown that cells exposed to a steady cAMP gradient, even when they are Latrunculin 
10 
A treated, following a brief global response, display a crescent of front-region markers toward 
11 
the high side of the gradient due to receptor-mediated activation of signaling events55, 56. As 
12 
shown in Figure 2H and Figure S4A, when Dictyostelium cells were exposed to a chemotactic 
13 
gradient, the charge sensor consistently moved away from the front region crescent and 
14 
accumulated at the back. We quantified the complementarity between charge sensor and PIP3 in 
15 
terms of CP index; in all these scenarios, the extent of complementary was found to be 
16 
comparable to established back protein, PTEN (Figure 2I; Figure S2D).  
17 
 
18 
These asymmetric distributions depended on charge and not on the specific amino acid 
19 
sequences of the sensor. As the positively charged arginines of the charge sensor were 
20 
sequentially replaced with neutral glutamines (Table S1), the distribution became increasingly 
21 
uniform over the cortex (Figures 2J-2L; Figures S4B-S4F). The dynamics of R(+2)-Pre sensor 
22 
(Figures 2J and 2K; Figures S4D and S4F) resembled the uniform distribution of surface receptor

Anionic lipids contribute to the surface charge in an integrative manner 
12 
 
13 
As multiple anionic lipids decreased during the activated phase, we tested whether any single 
14 
phosphoinositide fully accounted for the asymmetric distribution of the surface charge sensor.  
15 
When we rapidly depleted PI(4,5)P2 by recruiting Inp54p to the membrane, PHPLCδ, moved to 
16 
the cytosol (Figure 3A; Figure S6A), as expected. However, R(+8)-Pre remained membrane 
17 
bound (Figures 3B and 3C). As previously published9, 57, upon PI(4,5)P2 depletion, the cells 
18 
switched migration mode and displayed increased velocity (Figure 3D; Figures S6B and S6C). 
19 
Correspondingly, the front- and back- state regions expanded and contracted, respectively. 
20 
Nevertheless, R(+8)-Pre still faithfully localized to the back-state of the cortex (Figure 3E).  
21 
Similarly, in Dictyostelium Dd5p4– cells, where PI(3,4)P2 levels are low, front regions are 
22 
expanded, and migration is also altered 28, R(+8)-Pre remained on the membrane (Figures S6D

1 
cell (Figure 3F). To be certain that membrane retention of surface charge sensor following 
2 
recruitment of Inp54p was not due to its binding to the product PI4P, we recruited pseudojanin 
3 
by a similar chemically induced dimerization system. This particular chimera of INPP5E and 
4 
sac1 was reported to be sufficient to simultaneously convert PI(4,5)P2 to PI4P and PI4P to PI58. 
5 
As expected, we found that upon recruitment of pseudojanin from cytosol to membrane, 
6 
PI(4,5)P2 sensor PHPLCδ moved to cytosol (Figure 3G and Video S6) and so did PI4P-specific 
7 
sensor PHOsh2X2 (Figure 3H and Video S6). However, almost all of the R(+8)-Pre remained on 
8 
the membrane (Figures 3I and 3J; ; Video S7).  Together, these results demonstrate that the 
9 
dynamic regulation of the membrane surface charge depends on PI(4,5)P2 and PI(3,4)P2, but 
10 
there are additional key contributors, such as PS and PA, for example. 
11 
 
12 
 We sought a possible mechanism for the local decreases of PS on activated regions of inner 
13 
membrane since it is not readily modified by kinase/phosphatases like the phosphoinositides. 
14 
Reversible flipping of PS in non-apoptotic conditions has been previously reported in several 
15 
physiological contexts such as cytosolic calcium release and immunological stimulation59, 60. 
16 
However, despite multiple speculations over the years22, 47, whether rapid and dynamic 
17 
externalization can regulate local decreases in PS pertinent to signaling and cytoskeleton 
18 
activation has not been shown. We performed a transient Annexin-V binding assay (see methods 
19 
for details) to label outer leaflet PS during protrusion formation. Remarkably, we found that, on 
20 
the outer leaflet, PS strongly localizes to the protrusion or front-state regions(Figure 3K), closely 
21 
opposing its inner leaflet localization (Figure 1G-1I). We confirmed that in quiescent cells where 
22 
protrusions were absent, Annexin V did not bind to outer leaflet (Figure S6F). Pearson’s

7 
 
8 
As explained earlier, in the inner leaflet of the membrane, PI(3,4,5)P3 is present at negligible 
9 
amounts compared to other anionic phospholipids47, 51-53 and theoretically, it can perform its 
10 
well-known signaling functions without significant contribution to surface charge52. 
11 
Nevertheless, we directly tested the contribution of PIP3 to the membrane surface charge profile 
12 
using genetic and pharmacological perturbations. First, we treated the cells with LY294002, a 
13 
potent inhibitor of PI3K61-64, which resulted in a loss of PIP3 accumulation in the protrusions 
14 
(Figure 3M). Yet, the typical depletion of the surface charge sensor from the protrusions was 
15 
largely unchanged (Figure 3M). Second, we used Dictyostelium PI3K 1–/2– cells where PIP3 
16 
production is severely impaired62, 65. Observing the surface charge sensor profile in those cells 
17 
with respect to different front-state events such as F-actin polymerization (Figure 3N) and Ras-
18 
activation (Figure 3O), we again found that negative surface charge is consistently lowered 
19 
during signaling and cytoskeletal network activation, irrespective of PI(3,4,5) levels. These 
20 
results demonstrate that PIP3 does not significantly contribute to the membrane surface charge.

Decrease in net membrane surface potential is sufficient to trigger protrusions and 
1 
abrogate the pre-existing polarity 
2 
 
3 
The coordinated regulation of generic surface charge suggests that it is a key integrator of signal 
4 
transduction and cytoskeletal networks. Previously, the spatiotemporal behavior of these 
5 
networks has been modeled as an excitable network consisting of positive and delayed negative 
6 
feedback loops 9, 57, 66-69. Independent studies suggest that the delayed negative feedback loop 
7 
required for excitability includes the action of substrates of AKTs and possibly other refractory 
8 
state molecules57, 70. The positive feedback was implemented as a mutually inhibitory interaction 
9 
between front and back states, consisting of Ras/Rap and PI(4,5)P2 or PI(3,4)P2, respectively 9, 
10 
28, 57.  Our data suggest that, while decreases in specific lipids can be effective activators, 
11 
mutually inhibitory positive feedback loop may be controlled by overall surface charge (Figure 
12 
4A).  In this scenario, the activated state would decrease negative charge while decreasing 
13 
negative charge would lead to activation.  Having demonstrated the former, we next sought to 
14 
assess whether directly perturbing the negative surface charge on the membrane could alter the 
15 
strength of the loop and thereby affect protrusion formation, polarity organization, and migratory 
16 
behavior.  
17 
 
18 
To this end, we developed two non-specific biophysical optogenetic actuators designed to 
19 
acutely increase or decrease the negative surface potential profile.  Unlike previously reported 
20 
actuators that were developed based on specific components such as GEFs, GAPs, RGS, or 
21 
kinases/phosphatases 71-75, these unique actuators, consisting of a short chain of charged amino 
22 
acids, are not directed at any particular biochemical reaction.

The first actuator, designated Opto-ACTU+, had positive charge of +16 (Figure 4B). We 
2 
anticipated that when recruited from cytosol to the membrane in a spatially restricted fashion 
3 
(Figure 4C; Figure S7A), it would reduce the negative surface charge and thereby increase 
4 
positive feedback (Figure 4A). As a result, the threshold of the excitable networks would be 
5 
lower, the probability of spontaneous firing would increase, and consequently, the cell would 
6 
generate more protrusions de novo. Indeed, when Opto-ACTU+ was recruited to a quiescent 
7 
back region of a polarized Dictyostelium cell, new protrusions started forming nearby (Figure 
8 
4D; Figure S7B; Video S8), in a remarkably similar fashion as previously obtained by specific 
9 
biochemical perturbations such as optical activation of Rac1, Cdc42, or the GPCR opsins 76-78. 
10 
With recruitment of uncharged Opto-CTRL new protrusions rarely appeared near the irradiation 
11 
area (Figures S7C and S7D; Video S8), indicating that the protrusion formation is due to the 
12 
negative surface charge reduction and not light irradiation or cryptochrome recruitment. As the 
13 
angular histograms demonstrate, the probability of new protrusion generation was highest in the 
14 
vicinity of the Opto-ACTU+ recruitment (Figure 4E) whereas Opto-CTRL recruitment did not 
15 
induce a consistent bias (Figure S7E).  
16 
 
17 
We anticipated that when Opto-ACTU+ is globally recruited from cytosol to membrane in 
18 
polarized cells, more protrusions would be generated, and polarity would be disrupted. Indeed, 
19 
within few minutes of global recruitment of Opto-ACTU+, cells began to rapidly extend 
20 
protrusions all along the cortex (Figures 4F and 4G; Video S9), including from domains of 
21 
erstwhile back-states. Consequently, polarity was abrogated (Figure 4H) and migration was 
22 
impaired (Figures 4I and 4J).  The number of new protrusions increased around 2.5 fold (Figure

8 
Careful observation revealed that in addition to more protrusion formation induced by the 
9 
recruitment of the Opto-ACTU+, a series of events was occurring: As new protrusions/front-state 
10 
regions eastablished, recruited Opto-ACTU+ was quickly moving away from these regions 
11 
(Figures 4F and 4G; Video S9). In other words, once recruited, Opto-ACTU+ accumulated in a 
12 
back-state region, it triggered a protrusion there, and subsequently translocated away, 
13

DISSCUSSION 
6 
 
7 
Our study demonstrates that negative surface charge on the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane 
8 
spatiotemporally coordinates the components of the Ras/PI3K/TORC2/F-actin network that 
9 
control cell migration and polarity. We show that, under physiological conditions, the levels of 
10 
phosphoinositides PI(4,5)P2 and PI(3,4)P2 as well phosphatidylserine and phosphatidic acid on 
11 
the inner membrane decrease during network activation at membrane protrusions and front-state 
12 
regions on ventral surfaces of cells. These changes collectively account for a major reduction in 
13 
membrane surface charge or zeta potential which serves to integrate the signal transduction 
14 
events in those activated regions. Altering membrane surface charge is necessary and sufficient 
15 
for network activation and, not a merely a consequence of it, since recruiting a positively 
16 
charged actuator to the membrane activates the network whereas recruiting a negatively charged 
17 
actuator prevents the chemoattractant or growth factor stimulated activation. Our results suggest 
18 
that inner membrane surface charge is a key biophysical parameter that appears within the 
19 
feedback loops that determines the set-point of these networks.

Our data suggest that the fluid-mosaic model of the membrane is more complex than it is 
1 
originally envisioned. We find that the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane is constantly 
2 
remodeling in a highly coordinated manner. Large patches of multiple anionic lipids and 
3 
peripheral membrane proteins co-segregate into defined “phase” domains which propagate 
4 
dynamically. In the case of the phosphoinositides and many of the peripheral membrane proteins, 
5 
the propagation is not due to lateral translocation but rather a sequential transient biochemical 
6 
modification of headgroups of the lipids and shuttling of the proteins68, 86, 87.   Many of the 
7 
enzymes that modify the headgroups of phosphoinositides have been identified and for a handful 
8 
of cases, their specific activation cascade is known. For example, PIP3 levels on the membrane 
9 
increases when PI3K is activated by Ras88, 89 as well as when PTEN level decreases81, 90. 
10 
However, in-depth future studies will be needed to determine which specific kinases and 
11 
phosphatases are activated or inhibited in sequence to control the levels of PI(4,5)P2, PI(3,4)P2 
12 
or PA in membrane. Additionally, as our data suggest, the regulation of PS is possibly regulated 
13 
by dynamic externalization and hence we anticipate that several flippases, floppases, and 
14 
scramblases whose activation tracks closely with Ras/PI3K/TORC2/F-Actin network activation 
15 
and which co-segregate in front or back-state phase, will be identified in future. 
16 
 
17 
The optogenetic actuators we developed allowed us to alter inner membrane surface charge in a 
18 
generic fashion, inside an intact live cell, in a spatiotemporally restricted fashion. Thus, this 
19 
induced, biophysical change, i.e. a reduction in zeta potential, sets in motion a series of 
20 
biochemical events that were previously shown to be involved in migration, protrusion 
21 
formation, and polarity. Despite its implications, such an effect of surface charge perturbation on 
22 
different signaling and cytoskeletal events was hitherto largely unexplored, primarily due to

1 
approaches with isolated membranes 29, 31, 37, 91-94 or giant unilamellar vesicle (GUV)/liposome or 
2 
charged surfactant usually cannot accurately mimic the physiological environment of the cell due 
3 
to incorrect lipid compositions, lack of counterions, and/or compromised inner leaflet integrity. 
4 
Other recent methods22, 24, 47, 52, such as phosphoinositide degradation/synthesis by chemically 
5 
induced dimerization or pharmacological perturbations to increase cytosolic cation 
6 
concentrations or cellular ATP depletion do not provide any spatial control. Moreover, these 
7 
approaches do not decouple the surface charge from other cell physiological changes.  
8 
Optogenetics do provide spatial and temporal control inside intact live cells, but most have been 
9 
engineered to directly change the biochemical activity and/or localization of one particular 
10 
network component71, 72, 75.all of those were engineered to directly change the biochemical 
11 
activity and/or localization of one particular network component. As far as we know, the 
12 
synthetic optogenetic systems we have developed here are the first to assess the effect of 
13 
standalone surface charge perturbation in situ. Since these novel actuators can work orthogonally 
14 
to external cues such as chemoattractant gradients or external electric field, we envision that 
15 
these can potentially help in developing more in-depth molecular architecture of receptor 
16 
mediated biochemical network activation. We anticipate that in different physiological scenarios 
17 
such as immune synapse formation and phagocytosis where surface charge remodeling has been 
18 
reported (and others that likely will be found in the future), our tools will enable unique 
19 
perturbation strategies that can reveal intricate coordination pattern.

22 
cytoskeletal events that control cell migration and polarity.  We suggest the term “action surface

charge.  We observed that high negative surface charge corresponds to a “resting” or back-state 
2 
of the membrane while the regions of decreasing surface charge are transitioning to an 
3 
active/front-state which leads to protrusions. We envision that, as surface charge decreases, 
4

5 
are needed to determine which other crucial network components may be directly regulated by

7

8

9 
that enables small fluctuations such as a small initial drop in zeta potential to expand into 
10

11 
to the ability of transmembrane potential to regulate key ion channels which in turn regulates 
12 
transmembrane potential during action potential propagation.

14 
The “action surface potential” hypothesis can explain a series of heretofore puzzling 
15 
observations:  First, as we discussed, in most signal transduction networks a vast number of 
16 
activities undergo a highly coordinated stereotypical transient response.  This extraordinary 
17 
degree of coordination within an extensive series of stepwise interactions could be facilitated if 
18 
the regulation of key components depended on an organizer such as the surface charge. Second, 
19 
cells expend significant energy to sustain an asymmetric distribution of anionic lipids on inner 
20 
leaflet of the membrane, which has little apparent structural advantage. Of course, PIP2 serves as 
21 
a substrate for PLC and PI3K and PS as a signal for apoptosis. However, we suggest that 
22 
another, perhaps larger, role of this asymmetric distribution of charged lipids is to help in setting

15 
after 1-2 days, as per the antibiotic resistances of the vectors. For optogenetics and chemically-
16 
induced-dimerization experiments where three different protein co-expressions were necessary, 
17 
two different pCV5 vectors and one pDM358 vector were used and cells were selected with both 
18 
drugs.

states were restricted to be strictly nonnegative.
